content.text: What it is

Hardware requirements for running models locally describe the minimum and recommended specifications — primarily GPU VRAM, system RAM, storage throughput, CPU, and power supply — needed to load a given large language model and run inference at a useful speed on a personal workstation or on-premises server.
When practitioners talk about "running a model locally," they always hit the same ceiling first:
GPU VRAM
. A model's billions of floating-point parameters must reside somewhere in memory during inference, and which memory determines everything about speed, feasibility, and cost.
Why it matters

Misunderstanding hardware requirements is the most common reason a beginner's first local LLM experiment fails. You download a 14-GB model file, double-click it, and either get an out-of-memory error, a crash, or inference so slow it produces one token every few seconds. Understanding requirements in advance lets you choose the right model for your hardware — or choose the right hardware for a target model — before spending time or money.

For agentic AI workflows specifically, hardware requirements become more pressing because agents run many inference calls in sequence, sometimes with long contexts that grow the KV cache significantly. A setup that is borderline acceptable for a single short query may fail for a multi-turn planning loop running hundreds of iterations.
Benefits
Complete data privacy — prompts and responses never leave your machine, critical for medical, legal, and financial workloads.
Zero per-token cost after initial hardware purchase — batch workloads that would cost thousands of dollars via API are free at the margin.
Full control over model version, quantization level, and inference parameters (temperature, sampler, context length).
Offline operation — works without internet access, useful in air-gapped or secure environments.
Freedom to fine-tune and experiment without worrying about rate limits or content policies.
Reproducibility — the model weights are static files; inference output is deterministic for the same seed and settings.
Trade-offs
High upfront hardware cost — a capable consumer GPU (RTX 4090) costs around ,600 USD; professional data-center cards are far more.
Power consumption — modern GPUs draw 300–600 W under load, which translates to meaningful electricity costs and heat output.
Hard VRAM ceiling — you cannot run a model that exceeds your VRAM without quality-degrading quantization or slow CPU offloading.
Maintenance responsibility — driver updates, thermal management, and hardware failure are on you, not a provider.
Scaling is limited — a single local machine cannot serve thousands of concurrent users the way cloud infrastructure can.
Hardware depreciates — new GPU generations erode the performance advantage of today's purchase within two to three years.
How it works

When an LLM is loaded, the runtime reads its weight tensors from disk and places them into GPU VRAM. During each forward pass (one token generation step), the GPU performs massive matrix-vector multiplications across all transformer layers. Three components compete for VRAM:
Model weights
— the billions of parameters that define the model's knowledge. These are fixed after loading.
KV cache
— a growing buffer that stores key and value vectors for every token in the current context. It grows linearly with context length and can rival the weight size for long conversations.
Activations
— intermediate tensors produced during the forward pass. These are temporary but require a few hundred MB.
A practical formula for estimating total VRAM need:
VRAM ≈ (parameters × bytes_per_param) + KV_cache_GB + ~0.5 GB activations
Bytes per parameter depend on the numeric precision (quantization level):
FP16 / BF16 (16-bit): 2 bytes per parameter — a 7B model needs ~14 GB before the KV cache.
INT8 (8-bit): 1 byte per parameter — a 7B model needs ~7 GB.
INT4 / Q4 (4-bit): ~0.5 bytes per parameter — a 7B model needs ~3.5–4 GB.

Generation throughput (tokens per second) is primarily limited by GPU memory bandwidth — how fast the GPU can read the weights on each forward pass — rather than raw compute (FLOPS). A GPU with large but slow VRAM is slower than one with smaller but faster VRAM, all else equal.
Architecture

attrs.alt: Local LLM hardware architecture diagram showing Storage, System RAM, GPU, CPU, Power & Cooling, and output flow with VRAM requirements legend

content.text: The diagram traces the inference pipeline from left to right. Model weights are read from NVMe SSD into system RAM during loading, then transferred across the PCIe bus into GPU VRAM. Once there, the GPU's tensor cores execute each transformer layer in parallel. The CPU handles tokenization and sampling logic but is not on the hot inference path. Power and thermal design constrain sustained throughput — a GPU that thermal-throttles drops to a fraction of its rated bandwidth. Output tokens stream back to the application once the GPU decodes them.
When to use it
Running models locally is the right choice when one or more of the following apply:
Privacy requirements prohibit sending data to external APIs — regulated industries like healthcare (HIPAA), finance, and legal work.
High-volume workloads where per-token API costs would accumulate to thousands of dollars per month.
Offline or air-gapped environments — manufacturing floors, research vessels, classified facilities.
Research and experimentation where you need to modify model weights, test fine-tunes, or explore quantization effects without API permission.
Latency-critical applications that need sub-100ms first-token latency and cannot tolerate internet round-trips.
Agentic pipelines that run dozens of sequential inference calls per task — running locally avoids API rate limits and reduces per-step cost to zero.
When you have only occasional inference needs, a small team, or need frontier-scale models (200B+ parameters), cloud API access is usually the better choice.
How to implement
Step 1 — Audit your hardware
Run the hardware check script below to see your GPU VRAM and system RAM. Compare those numbers against the model's requirements at your preferred quantization level.
Step 2 — Choose a quantization level
Start with
Q4_K_M
(4-bit quantization with K-means, medium size). It fits in far less VRAM than FP16 with only modest quality loss on most tasks. Move up to
only if you notice quality problems.
Step 3 — Install a runtime
ollama
— easiest starting point; install, run , done.
llama.cpp
— maximum control; supports all quantization formats and partial GPU offloading.
vLLM
— best throughput for serving multiple concurrent requests.
Step 4 — Monitor on first run
Watch GPU VRAM usage with
nvidia-smi dmon -s mu
or
watch -n 0.5 nvidia-smi
. If VRAM is exhausted, the runtime either crashes, falls back to CPU offloading (very slow), or truncates the context.
Step 5 — Validate output quality
After switching quantization levels, run a short benchmark prompt and compare outputs. Q4 is usually acceptable; Q2 noticeably degrades coherence.

import subprocess
import sys

def check_hardware() -> dict:
    """Check available GPU VRAM and system RAM for local LLM inference."""
    results: dict = {}

    # --- GPU via nvidia-smi ---
    try:
        raw = subprocess.check_output(
            [
                "nvidia-smi",
                "--query-gpu=name,memory.total,memory.free",
                "--format=csv,noheader,nounits",
            ],
            text=True,
        )
        gpus = []
        for line in raw.strip().splitlines():
            name, total_mb, free_mb = [p.strip() for p in line.split(",")]
            gpus.append(
                {
                    "name": name,
                    "total_gb": int(total_mb) / 1024,
                    "free_gb": int(free_mb) / 1024,
                }
            )
        results["gpus"] = gpus
    except FileNotFoundError:
        results["gpus"] = []
        print("nvidia-smi not found — no NVIDIA GPU detected")

    # --- System RAM via /proc/meminfo ---
    try:
        with open("/proc/meminfo") as f:
            for line in f:
                if line.startswith("MemTotal"):
                    results["ram_gb"] = int(line.split()[1]) / (1024 ** 2)
                    break
    except OSError:
        results["ram_gb"] = 0.0

    return results


def estimate_vram(params_b: float, bits: int = 16, kv_cache_gb: float = 2.0) -> float:
    """Return estimated VRAM requirement in GB.

    Args:
        params_b:    Model size in billions of parameters (e.g. 7.0 for 7B).
        bits:        Quantization width — 16, 8, or 4.
        kv_cache_gb: KV-cache overhead at your target context length.
    """
    bytes_per_param = bits / 8
    weights_gb = params_b * 1e9 * bytes_per_param / (1024 ** 3)
    return weights_gb + kv_cache_gb


def can_fit(params_b: float, bits: int, available_gb: float) -> bool:
    needed = estimate_vram(params_b, bits)
    return available_gb >= needed


if __name__ == "__main__":
    hw = check_hardware()

    print("=== System Hardware ===")
    for g in hw.get("gpus", []):
        print(f"  GPU  : {g['name']}")
        print(f"         VRAM total : {g['total_gb']:.1f} GB")
        print(f"         VRAM free  : {g['free_gb']:.1f} GB")
    print(f"  RAM  : {hw.get('ram_gb', 0):.1f} GB")

    print("
=== VRAM Estimates ===")
    models = [
        ("Llama 3.1 8B",  8.0),
        ("Mistral 7B",    7.0),
        ("Llama 3.1 70B", 70.0),
    ]
    for name, p in models:
        fp16 = estimate_vram(p, bits=16)
        q8   = estimate_vram(p, bits=8)
        q4   = estimate_vram(p, bits=4)
        print(f"  {name:<20s}  FP16: {fp16:6.1f} GB  Q8: {q8:5.1f} GB  Q4: {q4:5.1f} GB")
Examples
Concrete VRAM requirements for popular open-weight models, at full precision and Q4 quantization, assuming 2 GB KV cache for a 2K-token context:
Llama 3.1 8B — FP16: ~18 GB / Q4_K_M: ~5 GB. Fits on an RTX 3070 (8 GB) at Q4, or an RTX 4090 (24 GB) at FP16.
Mistral 7B v0.3 — FP16: ~16 GB / Q4_K_M: ~4.5 GB. Similar to Llama 3.1 8B.
Llama 3.1 70B — FP16: ~142 GB / Q4_K_M: ~42 GB. Requires two or more RTX 3090s at Q4, or a single A100 80 GB at FP16.
Llama 3.1 405B — FP16: ~810 GB (data-center only) / Q4_K_M: ~245 GB. Needs a multi-GPU server with 4–8 H100s at any practical precision.
Phi-3 Mini 3.8B — FP16: ~8 GB / Q4: ~2.5 GB. Runs on a GPU with only 4 GB VRAM at Q4, or even Apple M-series unified memory.
Context length also matters. A 7B model at Q4 with a
128K-token context
needs an additional ~20–30 GB for the KV cache alone, far exceeding a single 24 GB GPU.

"""Measure inference speed with Ollama.

Requires: pip install ollama
Model must be pulled first: ollama pull llama3.1:8b
"""
import time
import ollama

MODEL = "llama3.1:8b"


def measure_inference(prompt: str, model: str = MODEL) -> dict:
    """Run a prompt and return timing statistics."""
    start = time.perf_counter()
    response = ollama.chat(
        model=model,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    )
    elapsed = time.perf_counter() - start

    tokens_out = response.get("eval_count", 0)
    return {
        "model": model,
        "elapsed_s": round(elapsed, 2),
        "tokens_generated": tokens_out,
        "tokens_per_second": round(tokens_out / elapsed, 1) if elapsed > 0 else 0,
        "content": response["message"]["content"],
    }


def compare_context_lengths(model: str = MODEL) -> None:
    """Show how KV-cache pressure affects throughput at different context lengths."""
    prompts = {
        "short (50 tok)": "What is a transformer model? Answer in one sentence.",
        "medium (500 tok)": "Explain the architecture of a transformer model in detail, "
                            "covering self-attention, multi-head attention, feed-forward "
                            "layers, layer normalization, positional encoding, and how "
                            "the encoder and decoder stacks interact.",
    }
    for label, prompt in prompts.items():
        result = measure_inference(prompt, model=model)
        print(f"  [{label}]")
        print(f"    Time   : {result['elapsed_s']}s")
        print(f"    Speed  : {result['tokens_per_second']} tok/s")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    print(f"Benchmarking {MODEL}
")
    result = measure_inference("Explain GPU VRAM in two sentences.")
    print(f"Model : {result['model']}")
    print(f"Time  : {result['elapsed_s']}s")
    print(f"Tokens: {result['tokens_generated']}")
    print(f"Speed : {result['tokens_per_second']} tok/s")
    print(f"Output: {result['content'][:120]}...")
    print()
    print("Context-length throughput comparison:")
    compare_context_lengths()
Real-world example

Consider a legal technology team building an agentic contract review pipeline. They need to process sensitive documents (no data can leave the network) and run 50–100 inference calls per document analysis loop. Their evaluation process:
Start with hardware audit: each workstation has an RTX 4090 (24 GB VRAM) and 64 GB system RAM.
Target model: Llama 3.1 70B for its strong instruction-following. At Q4_K_M it needs ~42 GB — exceeds a single 4090.
Decision: use Llama 3.1 8B at Q8_0 (~9 GB VRAM), which fits easily with room for 8K-token KV cache.
Runtime: Ollama running as a local service on each workstation, with a Python agentic loop calling the Ollama REST API.
Benchmark result: ~45 tokens/second on 4090 with Q8_0 8B — sufficient for near-real-time contract annotation.
For documents requiring deeper reasoning, they reserve one server with 2x RTX 3090 (48 GB combined via NVLink) for 70B Q4 runs.
This example shows how hardware requirements shape model selection and deployment topology — not the other way around.
Common mistakes
Downloading FP16 weights when VRAM is insufficient.
The download itself is slow (14–140 GB), and the load fails at runtime. Always check the Q4 or Q8 variant first.
Forgetting KV cache overhead for long contexts.
A model that fits at 2K tokens may OOM at 32K. Budget at least 1 GB per 4K tokens of context for a 7B model.
Treating system RAM as equivalent to VRAM.
CPU offloading moves some layers to system RAM and cuts inference speed by 10–100x. It is a fallback, not a design target.
Ignoring VRAM bandwidth vs. capacity distinction.
An older GPU with 48 GB of slow HBM may produce fewer tokens/second than a newer GPU with 24 GB of faster GDDR6X, even though it has more total VRAM.
Not accounting for the operating system and other GPU processes.
A desktop system running a GUI may already use 1–2 GB of VRAM for the display driver. Check free VRAM, not total VRAM.
Using Q2 quantization to squeeze a large model in.
Q2 (2-bit) models lose substantial coherence and factual accuracy. Use a smaller, higher-quality model instead.
Best practices
Start with Q4_K_M.
It hits the best quality-per-VRAM trade-off for most use cases. Only go to Q8 if you observe concrete quality problems.
Buy VRAM, not clock speed.
For LLM inference, more VRAM almost always beats a faster GPU with less VRAM. A 3090 (24 GB) beats a 4070 Ti (12 GB) for LLM work despite the latter being newer.
Use NVMe SSD for model storage.
A sequential read speed of 5+ GB/s reduces model load time from minutes (SATA SSD) to seconds.
Profile your target context length
before committing to a hardware configuration. Agentic loops often expand context further than expected.
Keep 20% VRAM headroom.
Running near the VRAM limit causes memory fragmentation and can cause OOM errors mid-session.
Enable Flash Attention where supported.
FlashAttention-2 reduces KV cache memory use by fusing attention operations, allowing longer contexts on the same hardware.
Monitor thermals continuously.
GPU thermal throttling silently reduces throughput. A sustained temperature above 83°C on an NVIDIA GPU causes clock reduction.
Performance & scalability

Token generation rate scales with GPU memory bandwidth first, then compute (FLOPS). This is because inference is memory-bandwidth-bound, not compute-bound: for each token, all model weights must be read from VRAM, but only a small amount of arithmetic is done per weight byte.
Key performance factors ranked by impact:
GPU VRAM bandwidth
— e.g., H100 (3.35 TB/s) vs RTX 4090 (1.008 TB/s). Higher bandwidth → more tokens/second.
Quantization level
— Q4 reads half the bytes of FP16 per weight, roughly doubling effective bandwidth.
Batch size
— running multiple requests together amortizes weight reads, improving throughput (not latency).
KV cache size
— larger KV cache occupies more VRAM, potentially forcing smaller batch sizes or shorter contexts.
PCIe bandwidth
— only relevant for multi-GPU setups or CPU offloading; does not affect single-GPU inference.
Scaling beyond a single GPU uses one of two approaches:
Tensor parallelism (NVLink/NVSwitch)
— splits individual weight matrices across GPUs. Requires high-bandwidth interconnect (NVLink); consumer 3090 cards support 2-GPU NVLink.
Pipeline parallelism (PCIe)
— assigns complete transformer layers to different GPUs. Works over PCIe but adds latency between stages.

Apple Silicon (M3/M4 series) uses unified memory architecture where CPU and GPU share the same physical memory pool. This means an M3 Max with 96 GB can load a 70B Q4 model without splitting hardware, though memory bandwidth (~400 GB/s) is lower than a high-end NVIDIA GPU (~1 TB/s).

"""Compare quantization levels using llama-cpp-python.

Requires: pip install llama-cpp-python
Download GGUF files from HuggingFace (e.g. bartowski/Meta-Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct-GGUF).
"""
import time
from pathlib import Path
from llama_cpp import Llama

# Map quantization label -> local GGUF file path
QUANT_MODELS = {
    "FP16 (full precision)": "models/llama-3.1-8b-instruct.fp16.gguf",
    "Q8_0 (8-bit)":          "models/llama-3.1-8b-instruct.Q8_0.gguf",
    "Q4_K_M (4-bit)":        "models/llama-3.1-8b-instruct.Q4_K_M.gguf",
}

PROMPT = "What is GPU VRAM and why does it matter for LLMs?"
MAX_TOKENS = 64


def benchmark_quant(path: str, n_gpu_layers: int = -1) -> dict:
    """Load a GGUF model and run one inference pass.

    Args:
        path:          Absolute path to the .gguf file.
        n_gpu_layers:  -1 offloads all layers to GPU; 0 = CPU only.
    """
    if not Path(path).exists():
        return {"error": f"File not found: {path}"}

    t0 = time.perf_counter()
    llm = Llama(
        model_path=path,
        n_gpu_layers=n_gpu_layers,
        n_ctx=2048,
        verbose=False,
    )
    load_time = time.perf_counter() - t0

    t1 = time.perf_counter()
    out = llm(PROMPT, max_tokens=MAX_TOKENS, echo=False)
    infer_time = time.perf_counter() - t1

    tok = out["usage"]["completion_tokens"]
    return {
        "load_s":    round(load_time, 2),
        "infer_s":   round(infer_time, 2),
        "tok_per_s": round(tok / infer_time, 1) if infer_time > 0 else 0,
        "output":    out["choices"][0]["text"].strip()[:120],
    }


if __name__ == "__main__":
    print(f"Prompt: {PROMPT}
")
    for label, path in QUANT_MODELS.items():
        print(f"--- {label} ---")
        r = benchmark_quant(path)
        if "error" in r:
            print(f"  Skipped: {r['error']}")
        else:
            print(f"  Load time : {r['load_s']}s")
            print(f"  Infer time: {r['infer_s']}s  ({r['tok_per_s']} tok/s)")
            print(f"  Output    : {r['output']}")
        print()
Tooling & ecosystem
The local LLM tooling ecosystem matured rapidly between 2023 and 2025. Key tools:
llama.cpp
— C++ inference engine by Georgi Gerganov. Supports GGUF quantization, Metal (Apple), CUDA, and CPU. The de-facto reference for portable local inference.
Ollama
— wraps llama.cpp behind a Docker-like CLI and REST API.  downloads and runs a model in seconds.
vLLM
— production-grade GPU inference server with PagedAttention for efficient KV cache management. Best for high-throughput serving.
LM Studio
— GUI application for Windows/Mac/Linux. Handles model discovery, download, and chat without command-line access.
HuggingFace Transformers
— full-featured Python library; supports any model architecture but requires more setup than Ollama.
ExLlamaV2
— highly optimized CUDA inference engine for NVIDIA GPUs; produces the fastest tokens-per-second for many consumer GPUs.
nvidia-smi
— NVIDIA's built-in CLI tool for monitoring GPU utilization, VRAM usage, temperature, and power draw. Essential for profiling.
nvtop
— terminal-based GPU process monitor similar to htop, showing per-process VRAM usage in real time.

is the best starting point. It runs any 7B–13B model at FP16 and 70B models at Q4, and Ollama makes setup trivial. Apple M-series machines are an excellent alternative for those who prioritize silence, power efficiency, and a laptop form factor.
Security & reliability
Running models locally offers significant security advantages over cloud APIs:
Complete data isolation.
Prompts, context, and outputs never leave your network. There is no provider who could log, train on, or disclose your data.
No API key exposure.
Cloud APIs require storing and rotating API keys. Local inference has no external credential to protect.
Static model weights.
The model does not change without your explicit action. Cloud models can be silently updated, changing behavior between calls.
Reproducible determinism.
With the same seed and quantization, output is reproducible — useful for testing and auditing agentic pipelines.
Reliability considerations:
Hardware failure is your responsibility.
GPUs fail, VRAM can develop errors, and there is no SLA. Plan for MTBF (mean time between failures) especially in production deployments.
Thermal throttling reduces throughput unpredictably.
Insufficient cooling causes clock-speed reduction under sustained load — particularly relevant for agentic loops that run the GPU at 100% for hours.
Power stability.
A high-end GPU draws up to 600 W. An undersized or aging PSU can cause system instability or unexpected shutdowns under load. Use a gold-rated PSU with at least 20% headroom above peak system draw.
Driver and software stack compatibility.
CUDA, ROCm (AMD), and Metal (Apple) backends can break with OS or driver updates. Pin driver versions in production environments.

For highly sensitive workloads, consider hardware-level attestation (e.g., NVIDIA Hopper Confidential Computing) which provides cryptographic guarantees that model inference runs in a trusted execution environment — an evolving area as of mid-2025.
Key takeaways
GPU VRAM is the single most important hardware factor for running LLMs locally. If the weights do not fit, nothing else matters.
Quantization (4-bit Q4_K_M) cuts VRAM requirements roughly in half compared to FP16, with acceptable quality loss for most tasks. Always try Q4 before assuming a model is out of reach.
Token generation speed is limited by GPU memory bandwidth, not FLOPS. A card with faster VRAM produces more tokens per second than one with more but slower VRAM.
KV cache grows with context length. A model that fits at 2K tokens may exhaust VRAM at 32K. Budget VRAM for your actual maximum context, not just model weights.
CPU offloading to system RAM is a last resort — it can be 10–100x slower than keeping all layers on GPU. Prefer a smaller model that fits in VRAM entirely.
NVLink-connected multi-GPU setups add VRAM linearly and scale throughput well; PCIe multi-GPU works but with higher inter-GPU latency.
Local inference provides complete data privacy, zero marginal inference cost, and deterministic output — meaningful advantages for agentic AI systems handling sensitive information.
For beginners, an RTX 4090 (24 GB) or Apple M3/M4 Max (96–128 GB unified) covers the widest range of practical open-weight models.
Mental model

Think of the GPU as a chef's countertop, VRAM as the counter space, and model weights as the ingredients. Everything you want to cook today must fit on the counter. Quantization is vacuum-packing the ingredients to take up half the space — some flavor is lost, but most of the dish is still excellent. If you run out of counter space and start using the pantry (system RAM), the chef has to run back and forth constantly, and cooking becomes painfully slow.

When evaluating whether a model will run on your hardware, ask two questions in order: (1) Do the weights fit in VRAM at this quantization level? (2) Does the KV cache for my target context length fit in the remaining VRAM? If both answers are yes, you are good. If either is no, quantize more aggressively or choose a smaller model.
